frameworks.name: Consumable Media Framework
frameworks.definition: A three-part model for membership businesses: sell the system (education) once up front, then supply the monthly consumable inputs that make the system work, reducing churn by ensuring members need the product every single month.

frameworks.name: Three Levels of Accountability
frameworks.definition: Peer-to-peer (shoulder-to-shoulder with someone at the same stage), alumni (one step ahead, cheerleading), and expert (has solved the problem hundreds of times) accountability layers sold as a retention and value-delivery system inside membership offers.

frameworks.name: Ink-Printer Revenue Model
frameworks.definition: Sell the machine (system, education) once; sell the ink (consumable inputs) repeatedly every month. The initial one-time sale enables a recurring revenue stream from the supplies the machine requires.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that education, as a standalone product, has a fundamental revenue ceiling: the moment a customer learns the skill, the educational product loses all value for them, making repeat purchase and retention near-impossible. This is why educational businesses struggle to generate recurring revenue from the same customer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: pure education product with no ongoing consumable component
customer fully acquires the skill taught
claims.mechanism: Once a learner has the skill, the pre-learning value (infinitely high) drops to approximately zero post-learning; there is no reason to repurchase what you already possess.
bounded_by: Does not apply when ongoing consumable inputs are bundled with the education
Formal education systems work around this by inventing infinite credential layers (bachelor's, master's, PhD)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the most important element of a membership or community business for driving down churn is having a consumable product — something members actively use and replenish every month — rather than static knowledge or access alone.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: membership or subscription business
goal is churn reduction
bounded_by: Adding more non-consumable items does not increase value and may decrease it by overwhelming members

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that in a membership business, the highest-leverage revenue architecture is to sell the system (the education or methodology) once at a premium, and then sell the monthly inputs that make the system work as a recurring subscription. The recurring payment is justified because the inputs are consumed and depleted each month, unlike knowledge.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: sell the system once, the inputs many times
conditions: community or membership business with an underlying methodology or system
inputs to the system are genuinely consumed monthly
claims.mechanism: Consumable inputs (ads, vetted deal lists, trend data) are depleted after use, so members must return each month to get new ones — creating natural recurring demand unlike knowledge which is retained.
bounded_by: Requires that the consumable inputs have demonstrably high standalone value
Does not apply to pure software tools where the value is access, not content

claims.text: Alex Hormozi describes that for Gym Launch, the consumable media product was pre-tested winning ad creatives: Gym Launch would spend approximately $50,000 testing 100 ads across 10 to 20 representative markets spanning different demographics and operator quality tiers, identify the top 5 winners, and deliver those to gym members monthly. Members paid for access to proven winners rather than investing their own money and time to test ads.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: gym franchise or licensing model with 700+ locations
members already know how to run ads operationally
claims.mechanism: By doing the risk-bearing (spending $50k on tests) and the work (scripting, shooting, editing, testing across representative markets), Gym Launch removed both the financial risk and execution burden from individual gym owners.
bounded_by: Required Gym Launch to front the capital and labor for testing
Value is predicated on members having the operational capability to run ads locally

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when measuring the value of a consumable membership deliverable, two components must be quantified: (1) the money the member would lose attempting to test and find what works on their own, and (2) the direct revenue gain from using the pre-vetted deliverable. The membership fee is justified by the sum of both components.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: membership with a consumable media component
member would incur testing costs without the service

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that adding more items to a membership offer that members do not actively use does not increase the offer's value — in many cases it decreases perceived value by creating cognitive overload. One highly-used consumable item is worth more than one used item plus four unused ones.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: membership or subscription product
items added do not have standalone monthly use cases
claims.mechanism: Unused items do not generate recurring perceived value; they may even signal complexity or wasted spend, increasing churn rather than reducing it.
bounded_by: Additional items that are themselves consumable and high-use can still add value

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that community itself is a consumable resource — members cannot exhaust it and declare they have consumed enough community — meaning community access, alongside other monthly consumables, can legitimately anchor recurring membership revenue. However, community alone may not command the price point operators desire, requiring supplemental consumables.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: community-based membership model
members perceive ongoing interaction as valuable
bounded_by: Community alone may have lower willingness-to-pay than operators want, requiring additional consumable components

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the highest-leverage form of consumable media is research, analysis, and distillation of information that saves the member time — specifically pre-chewing work the member would otherwise have to do themselves. The net gain to the member is time saved, which they can convert to revenue using the system you sold them previously.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: member has already acquired the underlying skill or system
inputs to the skill/system require recurring research or identification work
claims.mechanism: Research, analysis, and distillation convert time-intensive work (finding winning ads, vetting properties, identifying trending products) into a ready-to-use deliverable; members trade a monthly fee for time they would otherwise spend, and that time has computable dollar value.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that retaining an existing customer is significantly less work than acquiring a new one, and that solving for churn via consumable monthly value is a higher-leverage use of operator effort than running more customer acquisition.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: finding new customers — way more work
conditions: membership or subscription business with meaningful existing base

claims.text: Alex Hormozi frames membership value delivery as existing on a two-level hierarchy: Level 1 is figuring out the complex work for the member (doing the research, testing, vetting); Level 2 is figuring it out for them at scale — systematically delivering that pre-chewed output to an entire community, not just one individual.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: figuring it out for them, level one; figuring it out for them at scale, level two
conditions: community-based membership model with recurring deliverable

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that an accountability system is a viable and high-value consumable membership product because most people already know what they need to do but fail to execute — making accountability itself the bottleneck, not knowledge. In his weight loss business, he attributed approximately 75% of the product's perceived value to accountability rather than fitness or nutrition content.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: behavior-change or self-improvement membership
customers are knowledge-sufficient but execution-deficient
claims.mechanism: When the knowledge gap is already closed, accountability becomes the binding constraint on outcomes; delivering accountability (peer, alumni, expert layers) addresses the actual bottleneck rather than re-teaching content members already have.

numbers.axis: share of perceived product value in weight-loss offer

bounded_by: Applies primarily when the knowledge gap is already closed for the customer segment
Does not apply when the customer genuinely lacks the foundational knowledge to act

claims.text: Alex Hormozi describes a magical product as one where complexity happens invisibly in the background, and the member experiences only the frictionless output. He cites a $250M product executive's framework: the product must feel magical, meaning all hard work is invisible to the end user.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: consumer-facing product or membership deliverable
claims.mechanism: Members do not value effort visibility; they value frictionless outcomes. When complexity is absorbed by the provider and only the result is delivered, perceived value and willingness-to-pay increase.

examples.text: Alex Hormozi describes the Gym Launch consumable media model: Gym Launch spent roughly $50,000 renting models, shooting ad content, editing, and testing approximately 100 ads across 10 to 20 representative markets segmented by demographic and operator quality. The top 5 winners were then delivered monthly to gym members, who could either run those proven ads immediately or spend their own money trying to find winners independently. Hormozi frames this as handing members a winning scratch-off ticket to insert into a machine (the gym's ad infrastructure) they had already paid for once.

Alex Hormozi cites a wholesaling teacher who, instead of just teaching students how to find properties, had his team research, vet, and rate deals every month and compiled them into a PDF delivered to his community. All the student had to do was drive to the address. Hormozi notes that if a member closed even one deal per year from the list, it covered the entire annual membership fee — making the value proposition mathematically obvious.

Alex Hormozi describes a print-on-demand community with approximately 2% monthly churn. The consumable media was a weekly or monthly list of trending print-on-demand products and logos, researched and compiled by the community operator using their own tools. Members received a ready-to-use trend list rather than having to do their own product research. Hormozi cites the extremely low churn as evidence that this consumable media delivery model dramatically anchors member retention.

Hormozi describes the ink-printer metaphor to illustrate the system-plus-inputs model: you teach someone how to do hair extensions (sell the system once), and then they buy your hair extensions every month for their customers. Or you teach them how to sell supplements and then they purchase your supplements monthly for resale. The initial education sale is a one-time event; the recurring supply of inputs is the repeating revenue engine.

Hormozi contrasts the cookbook analogy against the consumable media model: most membership operators give members a cookbook (methodology) but leave them without flour, eggs, an oven, or pots and pans (the inputs). The gap between having the recipe and being able to execute it is where most members fail and churn. He argues the fortune is in closing that gap — figuring out the missing inputs and delivering them pre-packaged.

Alex Hormozi describes his gym accountability system as a three-tier product: peer-to-peer accountability (a fellow member at the same stage suffering the same challenges), alumni accountability (a member who has completed the journey and cheers from the finish line), and expert accountability (a coach who has helped thousands of similar clients and can diagnose and break through any plateau). He describes this as the sales pitch he used to sell the accountability system at his gyms, attributing 75% of perceived value to accountability versus fitness or nutrition content.

practices.text: Before adding new items to a membership offer, identify whether each item will be actively consumed on a monthly basis. If it will not be consumed, do not add it — unused items dilute perceived value rather than increasing it.

Map the work that members must currently do themselves to get value from your system, then take that work in-house: research, analyze, distill, and deliver the output as a monthly consumable. The deliverable should be immediately actionable with minimal additional effort from the member.

When designing a consumable media offering, identify both the cost the member saves by not having to test or research themselves, and the direct revenue the pre-vetted output generates. Use both figures to anchor the monthly price.

For behavior-change memberships (fitness, sales training, business coaching), diagnose whether your customers' primary bottleneck is knowledge or execution. If it is execution, build and pitch an accountability system — structured as peer-to-peer, alumni, and expert tiers — rather than adding more educational content.

Design consumable deliverables to feel magical: absorb all complexity internally so that the member receives only a ready-to-use output. The member should not see or feel the research, testing, and distillation work behind the deliverable.
